next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

By the time Sereya's old funeral ferry nosed into Halcote's loading basin, the town had lost the smell Lale remembered. Flour and brick dust were still in it somewhere, but the flood had worked both into a wet paste that coated the wharf planks and climbed the walls in pale streaks. The basin itself was fuller than it should have been. Water had backed up through the grain drains and the old quarry run, and every culvert mouth along the quay made the same thick breathing sound, as if the town had grown lungs in its foundations.

Halcote's people stood back from the edge. They did not want to seem frightened, which only made the fear look expensive. Men in mill aprons held their own elbows. Women with market shawls tucked their hands under their arms and stared at the ferry as if rot might jump the gap. A mule cart had been left crooked in the lane with one wheel sunk to the axle in mud. The driver had apparently gone off in a hurry and forgotten the carrots still tied in a bunch behind the seat. One of them had slipped loose and floated in the gutter water like a severed orange finger.

Lale saw Oren Pike before he saw her. He had his waxed hat on, though the brim had gone soft in the rain, and he was biting a clove the way he always did when he wanted to smell wealthier than the room allowed. He kept one hand under his ledger satchel, protecting it from the weather with more tenderness than most men here gave their children. Two years ago he had haggled her down over shroud cloth and then praised her accounts in the same breath, a neat inland trick that had a...
open_hooks_to_carry: The chapter centers bodily care rather than tactical progress.
Riva and Lale finally speak honestly about leaving Nami behind after Sereya's death.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...y ledger he trusted.

By full dark the ropehouse fire had burned down to steaming black ribs. The yard stank of wet ash and cooked hemp. The opened fourth cut pulled clean now, almost innocent in its new sound. That was the delta's filthiest trick. Once damage had been paid for, water always pretended it had wanted order all along.

They loaded the funeral ferry in silence. One coil. Two poles. Blankets. Ledger. Food. Medicine. The little that was left of the mission fit too easily.

When Iven stepped into the narrow hull at last, it rocked under him with the intimate balance of a craft built for the dead. Enver stood on the dock with his leg bound rough, one soot-black hand on a mooring post. Riva took the stern. Daro and Lale settled Nami low in the middle. No one looked bigger in that boat than grief.

Iven set the salvaged pole against the gunwale and glanced once toward the place where the Bitter Thread had gone down. He could not see the wreck now. Only rain stippling the cut and the occasional drift of black char moving seaward.

He had wanted shame without witnesses all his life. It turned out shame was still shame when everybody saw it, only heavier, easier to point at, more expensive. It had a bank. A burned house. A father with his face in a rougher arrangement. A sister's boat on the riverbed.

Riva shoved them off.

The funeral ferry slipped into the opened water like a blade entering old scar tissue. Behind them Coil Reach smoked in the rain. Ahead lay less boat, less gear, less room for performance. Iven put both blistered hands on the pole anyway and pushed.
state_deltas_required: Nami's fever, physical exhaustion, and the possibility that the journey is now impossible.
The siblings choose to continue on Sereya's old hand-poled funeral ferry, bound by care and shared damage rather than confidence.
